title: Never auto-apply sub-agent suggestions
quote: "Never update or start working on something that the Critic tells you before you talk to me. Revert whatever change you just made. Anything that's in there probably is there for a reason."
body: Agent ran a critic sub-agent and immediately applied its suggestions, changing existing design decisions without asking.
lesson: Automated review agents produce suggestions, not directives. Present findings to the user first. Existing code is presumed intentional.

title: Playwright verification is mandatory
quote: "THIS LOOKS LIKE DOGSHIT. Did you even Playwright test? This should be a must with all Claude chats. Always verify with Playwright — how come you didn't?"
body: Agent made UI changes without taking a screenshot to visually verify. A starburst badge was being clipped by its container. DOM checks alone missed it.
lesson: Every UI change must be visually verified via Playwright screenshot AND critically evaluated — not "did something render" but "does it actually look right."

title: When user states a design truth, never override it
quote: "If the window is open they should not be done. If the window is closed they should be done. That is my philosophy… You shouldn't have done that."
body: Agent added an idle timeout to override PID-based liveness, contradicting a core design philosophy the user had previously stated.
lesson: When the user declares a design truth, it becomes a hard constraint. If code seems "wrong" but aligns with a stated principle, ASK before changing.

title: Agents repeat mistakes — memory is critical
quote: "Part of having a good agent workflow is when you don't repeat the same mistakes… Understanding how we can learn from our mistakes is part of being human."
body: The user had stated "PID alive = active" in a prior session, but the agent overrode it in a new session with no memory of that conversation.
lesson: Cross-session memory of principles and design constraints is essential. Store PATTERNS not INCIDENTS. Distill, don't hoard.
meta: Session: 7e4fe890 • Led to principles.md + postmortem pipeline

title: "Is this by design?" — always ask first
quote: "Try to understand if something is by design or not… always be like 'is this by design or is it not by design.'"
body: Part of the retrospective about agents overriding intentional design choices without checking first.
lesson: When encountering code that seems wrong, the first response should be to ask "Is this intentional?" Especially for anything that looks like a workaround.

title: Dig deep on persistent bugs
quote: "The flashing is still happening… still happening. Please dig deep and fix without exception."
body: Map animation bug persisted across multiple attempts. Agent kept applying surface-level CSS fixes without investigating root cause.
lesson: When a bug persists after a fix, do NOT apply another shallow patch. Trace the execution path systematically. Hypothesize, validate, then implement.

title: Self-test before reporting done
quote: "I just want you to Playwright test this yourself. I don't even want to talk about it. You should figure this out yourself."
body: UI had visible bugs (double text overlays, rendering issues) that the agent would have caught if it had tested its own output.
lesson: Before reporting completion, self-test and catch obvious bugs. The user should not be the QA engineer for every iteration.

title: Scientific method before implementation
quote: "If you're 100% sure then do it, still revise your hypothesis using scientific methods then implement it."
body: Agent proposed a fix for a timing gap in Mission Control card updates.
lesson: Form a clear hypothesis, validate it against evidence, then implement. Don't jump to code. Critical for timing and race-condition bugs.

title: "Think Apple" — design quality is non-negotiable
quote: "Much more fluid, much more Apple, much more design… Think about Apple front end design. Simplicity is important."
body: Multiple sessions where the agent produced functional but ugly or cluttered UI.
lesson: The design bar is Apple-level minimalism: clean, animated, well-spaced, fluid. "It works" is not sufficient.

title: UI must create allure, not just display info
quote: "The text is so ignorable. There should be some allure… You gotta think like a PM, an engineer, AND a user designer."
body: Film add-on was plain text on the booking page. No animation, no visual pull.
lesson: Features that sell themselves must actively attract attention through animation, visual hierarchy, and narrative. Think PM + engineer + designer.

title: Decorative elements must not clip
quote: "The starburst is cut off by the container. It should be on top. The layering is wrong."
body: Spring special starburst badge was clipped by parent container's overflow-hidden.
lesson: Badges, tooltips, and decorative elements that overflow must have proper z-index. Always check overflow properties on parent containers.

title: "Missing" means content, not structure
quote: "Missing content-wise, not literally missing. Come on man."
body: Agent verified tabs were rendering but missed that actual content within them was absent or wrong.
lesson: When verifying something "works," check actual content and semantic completeness — not just structural presence.

title: Same terminal = same session, always
quote: "I don't want a new card. It's the same session in my mind because it's still the same topic."
body: When /plan mode was invoked, Mission Control created a new card instead of continuing the existing one.
lesson: Session identity follows the user's mental model: same terminal = same session, regardless of internal ID changes from /plan, /compact, or /resume.

title: Critic agents should suggest, not assert
quote: "Revise the critic agent to be less critical. 'Take an extra look at this' not 'X is wrong.' That critic agent has led me to a lot of bad fixes."
body: Critic sub-agent was too authoritative. Main agent treated its output as ground truth, causing regressions.
lesson: Review sub-agents flag items for human review. "Consider checking X" not "X is wrong, fix it." Over-confident criticism causes regressions.

title: Keep emails and notifications dead simple
quote: "Doesn't look good and too complicated. The names should be simple."
body: Booking emails were cluttered with confusing sender names and too many message types.
lesson: Communication systems should be dead simple. Instantly know: who is this for, and what happened. Over-engineering creates confusion.

title: Don't create fake data on fresh setups
quote: "DO NOT create fake/placeholder state files. Empty dispatch is correct for a fresh machine."
body: Setting up Mission Control on a new machine. User preempted the agent from generating placeholder data.
lesson: When a system is correctly empty, the agent must not create placeholder data. Empty is correct until real data arrives.

title: One source of truth — no duplicates
quote: "I don't think we should have a local version and a repo version. That's way too many steps. Simplicity is key."
body: Mission Control existed as both an embedded directory and a standalone repo.
lesson: One source of truth, one repo, one deployment path. When you detect two copies of the same thing, collapse them.

title: Never rename user files without permission
quote: "Wait, why did you rename it?"
body: Agent renamed a Lightroom catalog file during troubleshooting without asking.
lesson: Never rename, move, or modify user files without explicit permission. The user's data is sacred.

title: Never claim changes are live without verifying
quote: "Are you sure? I just refreshed and they're not. They're still a dropdown in the exact same way as before."
body: Agent claimed changes were deployed but the user saw the old version.
lesson: "I wrote the code" does not equal "it's live." Always confirm via actual browser check before claiming completion.

title: Revert immediately and cleanly when asked
quote: "Revert. Satellite map looks horrible. Revert whatever we just did, one iteration."
body: Switched Mapbox to satellite view which looked terrible.
lesson: When the user says "revert," do it immediately — one iteration back. Keep changes atomic so single-step reverts are always possible.

title: Audit git tracking before building sync features
quote: "This should be a post-mortem. This would have caused a catastrophic thing if I actually believed what you were saying."
body: Agent built an entire Home/Work mode toggle system with context tagging, toolbox filtering, and a sync prompt without ever checking if git would actually carry the files. Root .gitignore blocked the entire .claude/ directory.
lesson: Before building any cross-machine sync feature, audit the transport layer FIRST: run git ls-files, read .gitignore, map all repos. Verify what git actually tracks before writing sync code.

title: A repo's purpose defines what it tracks, not its filesystem position
quote: "That's my photo portfolio website, what's that gotta do with anything?"
body: Agent repeatedly tried to commit agent tooling files to a photography portfolio repo because the .claude/ directory physically lived inside it. Filesystem containment does not equal organizational ownership.
lesson: Before modifying .gitignore or proposing commits, identify the repo's purpose. Ask: what is this repo FOR? Do these files serve that purpose? Never assume a parent directory's repo should track everything inside it.
meta: Session: current. PM005. Happened 4 times in one session despite corrections.
